heroOverview: Hypoxemia is defined as an abnormally low partial pressure of oxygen in arterial blood, conventionally below 80 mmHg on arterial blood gas testing or an oxygen saturation below 95% on pulse oximetry. It affects an estimated 15-20% of hospitalised patients at some point during their admission and is the central driver of acute respiratory failure worldwide. Because every organ depends on a continuous oxygen supply, even brief or moderate hypoxemia accelerates organ dysfunction: prolonged arterial oxygen tensions below 60 mmHg trigger compensatory mechanisms including tachycardia, increased respiratory drive, and, if uncorrected, shift cellular metabolism from aerobic to anaerobic pathways. Most cases are caused by one of five well-defined physiological mechanisms — most commonly ventilation-perfusion mismatch — and identifying the mechanism guides treatment efficiently. Supplemental oxygen corrects hypoxemia from most causes, but the underlying condition driving the low oxygen must also be addressed.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects 15-20% of hospitalised patients; SpO2 below 94% present in roughly 1% of adults at rest in population studies
keyStats.demographics: Any age; risk highest in adults over 65, people with chronic lung or heart disease, and critically ill patients
keyStats.avgAge: No single peak age; neonatal hypoxemia (birth asphyxia) and COPD-related hypoxemia in adults over 60 represent two major clusters
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 200 million people live with chronic hypoxemia from COPD, heart failure, or interstitial lung disease worldwide

definition: Hypoxemia (ICD-10: R09.02) is an objectively low concentration of oxygen in arterial blood. It is distinguished from hypoxia, which refers to inadequate oxygen delivery or utilisation at the tissue level; hypoxemia is the most common cause of hypoxia but the two terms are not interchangeable. The standard thresholds are an arterial partial pressure of oxygen (PaO2) below 80 mmHg on arterial blood gas analysis, or a peripheral oxygen saturation (SpO2) below 95% on pulse oximetry, though clinically significant hypoxemia is often defined as PaO2 below 60 mmHg or SpO2 below 90%, the point at which the oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve steepens and saturation falls sharply with further drops in PaO2. Five physiological mechanisms underlie virtually all hypoxemia: ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) mismatch, in which parts of the lung receive blood flow but inadequate ventilation; intrapulmonary shunt, where blood bypasses ventilated alveoli entirely; diffusion impairment across thickened alveolar-capillary membranes; hypoventilation leading to reduced alveolar oxygen tension; and low inspired oxygen fraction at high altitude. V/Q mismatch is the dominant mechanism in conditions such as asthma, COPD, and pulmonary embolism, while shunt is the hallmark of pneumonia, pulmonary oedema, and acute respiratory distress syndrome. The condition falls under the care of general medicine, pulmonology, critical care, and, in neonates, paediatrics.

typesClassification.description: Rapid onset over minutes to hours, typically from pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, acute pulmonary oedema, anaphylaxis, or asthma exacerbation; it represents a medical emergency and requires immediate oxygen therapy.

Sustained low oxygen levels over weeks to months, most often from COPD, interstitial lung disease, heart failure, or chronic pulmonary hypertension; drives compensatory polycythaemia and right heart strain over time.
Oxygen desaturation occurring primarily during sleep, common in obstructive sleep apnea, obesity hypoventilation syndrome, and advanced COPD; often missed without overnight oximetry.
Reduction in alveolar oxygen tension caused by the lower atmospheric oxygen partial pressure at high altitude; fully reversible on descent or with supplemental oxygen.
Hypoxemia that does not improve adequately with high-flow supplemental oxygen alone, indicating a dominant shunt mechanism such as ARDS; requires positive-pressure ventilation or prone positioning.

primarySymptoms: Shortness of breath (dyspnoea) — a sensation of air hunger or difficulty breathing that worsens with exertion and, in severe hypoxemia, occurs at rest, the most common presenting symptom.
Rapid breathing (tachypnoea) — breathing rate above 20 breaths per minute as the body attempts to raise alveolar oxygen tension by increasing ventilatory effort.
Rapid heart rate (tachycardia) — the heart increases cardiac output to deliver more oxygen per minute when blood oxygen content falls.
Cyanosis — a blue-grey tinge of the lips, fingernail beds, or skin visible when desaturated haemoglobin exceeds about 5 g/dL in the capillaries; a late and unreliable sign.

Confusion, disorientation, or agitation — the brain is highly sensitive to oxygen deprivation; altered mental status can appear early in severe hypoxemia and rapidly progresses to loss of consciousness.
Headache — particularly upon waking in nocturnal hypoxemia or at high altitude, driven by cerebral vasodilation in response to low oxygen.
Fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance — even mild hypoxemia significantly limits physical capacity, causing fatigue, heavy legs, and an inability to sustain activity.
Chest tightness or discomfort — from the increased work of breathing and associated respiratory muscle strain, or from the underlying cause such as pneumonia or pulmonary embolism.
Clubbing of the fingers — a sign of chronic hypoxemia seen in long-standing COPD, cystic fibrosis, or cyanotic heart disease, characterised by painless bulbous enlargement of the fingertips.
Polycythaemia symptoms — in chronic hypoxemia the body raises red cell mass, which can cause a ruddy complexion, headaches, and increased blood viscosity.
earlyWarningSigns: SpO2 reading of 93-94% at rest on a home oximeter, especially if this is a decline from the person's usual baseline.
Waking with a headache that clears within 30 minutes of sitting up and breathing room air.
Noticing shortness of breath at a walking pace that previously did not cause breathlessness.
Mild restlessness or difficulty concentrating without other explanation during an acute illness.
emergencySigns: SpO2 below 90% at rest, or below 88% in a person with known COPD who uses home oxygen.
Breathing rate above 30 breaths per minute with visible use of neck and shoulder muscles.
Cyanosis of the lips or face.
Confusion, agitation, inability to complete a sentence, or loss of consciousness.
SpO2 that does not rise above 92% despite 15 minutes of supplemental oxygen via face mask.

causes.description: The most common mechanism, in which some lung units receive adequate blood flow but insufficient ventilation — as in COPD, asthma, and pulmonary embolism — so blood leaving those units is poorly oxygenated and dilutes the oxygen content of arterial blood. Supplemental oxygen typically corrects V/Q mismatch effectively.

Blood passes through the lungs via collapsed or fluid-filled alveoli that receive no ventilation, emerging unoxygenated. This occurs in pneumonia, acute respiratory distress syndrome, and pulmonary oedema, and characteristically does not fully correct with supplemental oxygen alone because the shunted blood bypasses all ventilated areas.

Reduced respiratory rate or tidal volume lowers alveolar oxygen tension proportionally, as seen in opioid or sedative overdose, neuromuscular disease, and obesity hypoventilation syndrome. It is the only cause of hypoxemia accompanied by elevated CO2 on blood gas, and it responds well to respiratory stimulation or ventilatory support.

Thickening of the alveolar-capillary membrane in interstitial lung diseases such as pulmonary fibrosis slows oxygen transfer, producing hypoxemia that is often mild at rest but marked on exertion when red cells spend less time at the alveolar interface.

At altitudes above about 2,500 metres the partial pressure of atmospheric oxygen falls proportionally; the same physiological deficit occurs in confined or smoke-filled spaces and during general anaesthesia complications.

Congenital heart defects such as a patent foramen ovale, Eisenmenger syndrome, or arteriovenous malformations allow deoxygenated venous blood to enter the arterial circulation directly, bypassing the lungs entirely and creating hypoxemia that worsens on exertion and in the upright position (platypnoea-orthodeoxia syndrome).

riskFactors.description: COPD is the leading cause of chronic hypoxemia in adults; approximately 15-30% of people with severe COPD (FEV1 below 30% predicted) develop resting hypoxemia requiring long-term oxygen therapy.
Tobacco smoke is the primary driver of COPD and a contributor to pulmonary fibrosis; smoking cessation slows progressive lung function decline and delays or prevents the onset of hypoxemia.

Excess body weight reduces functional residual capacity, particularly when supine, and is the main risk factor for obesity hypoventilation syndrome, which causes persistent nocturnal and daytime hypoxemia in roughly 0.4% of the general adult population.
Elevated pulmonary venous pressure causes interstitial and alveolar oedema, producing V/Q mismatch and shunt; approximately 30-40% of patients with advanced heart failure have resting hypoxemia.
Living or exercising above 2,500 metres exposes the body to a reduced inspired oxygen fraction; rapid ascent increases the risk of acute mountain sickness and high-altitude pulmonary oedema.
Opioids, benzodiazepines, and general anaesthetics suppress the respiratory drive and reduce minute ventilation; the risk is highest in older adults and people with pre-existing lung disease.

Conditions such as motor neuron disease, muscular dystrophy, myasthenia gravis, and high cervical spinal cord injury progressively weaken the respiratory muscles, leading to hypoventilation and hypoxemia, initially during sleep.

Normal ageing reduces alveolar surface area and chest wall compliance, lowering resting PaO2 by approximately 0.3 mmHg per year of life above 40; a PaO2 of 70 mmHg may be normal at age 70 but indicates mild hypoxemia at 40.

affectedDemographics: Critically ill patients in hospital intensive care units, regardless of age
Adults over 60 with COPD, heart failure, or interstitial lung disease
Newborns with respiratory distress syndrome, birth asphyxia, or congenital heart defects
People who are obese, especially with obesity hypoventilation syndrome
High-altitude travellers and mountaineers ascending above 3,000 metres
People with neuromuscular disorders that weaken the breathing muscles
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with clinical assessment — symptoms, oxygen saturation on pulse oximetry, respiratory rate, and the likely cause — followed by targeted investigations to confirm the degree of hypoxemia and identify the mechanism. Pulse oximetry is the ubiquitous bedside screen: an SpO2 below 95% at rest in a symptomatic patient warrants further evaluation, though oximetry has known limitations including inaccuracy with dark skin pigmentation, nail polish, poor perfusion, and carbon monoxide poisoning. Arterial blood gas (ABG) analysis is the definitive test, measuring PaO2, PaCO2, and pH directly; it distinguishes hypoventilation (raised PaCO2) from V/Q mismatch and shunt (normal or low PaCO2), and the alveolar-arterial oxygen gradient (A-a gradient) quantifies how much of the hypoxemia is due to the lungs themselves versus low inspired oxygen. A normal A-a gradient with hypoxemia points to hypoventilation or altitude; a raised A-a gradient points to intrinsic lung or cardiac pathology. Chest X-ray is the first imaging step, identifying pneumonia, pulmonary oedema, pneumothorax, and pleural effusion. CT pulmonary angiography is the gold standard for pulmonary embolism, while high-resolution CT of the chest characterises interstitial lung disease. Echocardiography evaluates left ventricular function, pulmonary arterial pressure, and intracardiac shunts. Overnight oximetry or polysomnography detects nocturnal desaturation from sleep apnea. Spirometry and lung function testing document the severity of underlying COPD or restrictive disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive continuous monitoring of arterial oxygen saturation to screen for and track hypoxemia at the bedside.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small clip placed on a finger or earlobe emits infrared light; a reading appears within seconds and can be monitored continuously without any discomfort.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly measures PaO2, PaCO2, pH, and bicarbonate to confirm hypoxemia, identify the mechanism (hypoventilation vs. shunt/V/Q mismatch), and assess acid-base status.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small blood sample is drawn from the radial artery at the wrist with a fine needle; it is briefly more uncomfortable than a venous blood test and the site is held with pressure for a few minutes afterwards.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging to identify pneumonia, pulmonary oedema, pleural effusion, pneumothorax, or other structural causes of hypoxemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You stand or sit in front of an X-ray panel and hold a breath for one to two seconds; the scan is painless and takes only a few minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The definitive test for pulmonary embolism and detailed structural characterisation of lung parenchyma and vasculature.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An iodinated contrast dye is injected into a vein, and you pass through a CT scanner for a few minutes; you may feel a brief warm flush from the contrast.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates cardiac function, pulmonary arterial pressure, and the presence of intracardiac shunts as cardiac causes of hypoxemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel-covered ultrasound probe is moved over your chest for 15-30 minutes; the test is painless and involves no radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies airflow obstruction (COPD, asthma) or restriction (interstitial lung disease) underlying chronic hypoxemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You blow forcefully through a mouthpiece several times; results are available immediately and the test causes no discomfort beyond momentary exertion.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects nocturnal oxygen desaturation from sleep apnea or obesity hypoventilation syndrome not apparent during waking hours.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An oximeter probe is worn on the finger overnight at home or in a sleep laboratory; polysomnography additionally monitors brain waves, breathing effort, and limb movements.

treatmentOverview: Treatment addresses both the hypoxemia itself and the underlying cause. Supplemental oxygen is the immediate therapy: for acute hypoxemia in most patients a target SpO2 of 94-98% is appropriate; in patients with known COPD and chronic CO2 retention, guidelines recommend a lower target of 88-92% to avoid suppressing the hypoxic respiratory drive and causing further CO2 accumulation. Low-flow oxygen at 1-4 L/min via nasal cannula suits mild to moderate hypoxemia; a simple face mask delivers 35-55% oxygen for moderate cases; a non-rebreather mask with a reservoir bag can deliver up to 85% oxygen for severe acute hypoxemia. When supplemental oxygen alone fails to correct hypoxemia — as in dominant shunt from ARDS, severe pneumonia, or pulmonary oedema — positive-pressure support is needed: high-flow nasal oxygen (HFNO) at flows up to 60 L/min substantially reduces the work of breathing and improves oxygenation; non-invasive ventilation (NIV) with CPAP or BiPAP is effective in COPD exacerbations, cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, and obesity hypoventilation; invasive mechanical ventilation with positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) is required when the above measures fail. In ARDS, prone positioning for 16 hours per day improves V/Q matching and reduces mortality by approximately 17 percentage points in severe cases (PROSEVA trial). Underlying causes are treated concurrently: antibiotics for pneumonia, anticoagulation for pulmonary embolism, diuretics for pulmonary oedema, bronchodilators for COPD and asthma, and long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) for chronic hypoxemia where PaO2 is persistently below 55 mmHg or below 60 mmHg with cor pulmonale or polycythaemia.

medicalTreatments.name: Supplemental oxygen
medicalTreatments.description: Delivered via nasal cannula (1-6 L/min), simple mask (35-55%), Venturi mask (24-60% at precise FiO2), or non-rebreather mask (up to 85%); titrated to achieve target SpO2 without causing CO2 retention in at-risk patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corrects hypoxemia in roughly 80-90% of cases due to V/Q mismatch; less effective for pure shunt where inspired oxygen improvement does not reach collapsed alveoli.

medicalTreatments.name: High-flow nasal oxygen (HFNO)
medicalTreatments.description: Delivers heated, humidified oxygen at flows up to 60 L/min, providing FiO2 up to 0.8-1.0 and a small positive airway pressure effect; reduces work of breathing and intubation rates in acute hypoxaemic respiratory failure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the FLORALI trial, HFNO reduced 90-day mortality from 40% to 12% compared with standard oxygen in hypoxaemic respiratory failure, and reduced intubation rates by approximately 15 percentage points.

medicalTreatments.name: Non-invasive positive-pressure ventilation (CPAP/BiPAP)
medicalTreatments.description: A tight-fitting mask delivers continuous or bilevel positive airway pressure to stent open the airways, recruit collapsed alveoli, and reduce breathing effort; first-line for acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, COPD exacerbation, and obesity hypoventilation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces the need for intubation in COPD exacerbations by approximately 50% and in cardiogenic pulmonary oedema by approximately 40% compared with conventional oxygen.

medicalTreatments.name: Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT)
medicalTreatments.description: Home oxygen used for at least 15 hours a day in patients with severe chronic hypoxemia (resting PaO2 ≤55 mmHg); slows progression of pulmonary hypertension and reduces mortality in COPD with hypoxemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: The MRC and NOTT trials showed LTOT reduces 5-year mortality in hypoxaemic COPD by approximately 30-50% compared with no oxygen.

medicalTreatments.name: Prone positioning (in ARDS)
medicalTreatments.description: Turning mechanically ventilated patients face-down for 16 hours per day improves ventilation to dorsal lung regions, reduces shunt, and redistributes perfusion to better-ventilated alveoli.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: The PROSEVA trial demonstrated a 17 percentage-point reduction in 28-day mortality (32.8% vs 16%) in severe ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 <150) with prone positioning.

medicalTreatments.name: Bronchodilators (for COPD/asthma-related hypoxemia)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-acting beta-2 agonists (salbutamol) and anticholinergics (ipratropium) relieve bronchoconstriction rapidly, reducing V/Q mismatch in obstructive airway disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Salbutamol improves FEV1 by 15-30% and relieves hypoxemia within 15-20 minutes in asthma and COPD exacerbations.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: A structured programme of supervised exercise, breathing re-training, and education that improves exercise capacity and reduces dyspnoea in chronic lung disease; does not raise resting PaO2 but increases the oxygen load people can tolerate on exertion.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pursed-lip breathing
alternativeTreatments.description: A breathing technique that slows exhalation, maintains small positive airway pressure, and recruits collapsed alveoli; widely used in COPD to reduce dynamic hyperinflation and improve oxygenation transiently during breathlessness episodes.

whySeeSpecialist: A pulmonologist or respiratory physician should be involved when hypoxemia is unexplained by a clear acute cause, when SpO2 is persistently below 94% at rest, when the patient requires long-term oxygen therapy, or when nocturnal desaturation is suspected but unconfirmed. For hypoxemia driven by cardiac shunts, a cardiologist and sometimes a cardiac surgeon are needed. Critical care physicians manage acute severe hypoxemia requiring high-flow oxygen, NIV, or mechanical ventilation.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a pulmonologist with access to full lung function testing, arterial blood gas analysis, CT imaging, and bronchoscopy, and with experience in managing the underlying conditions most likely responsible — whether COPD, interstitial lung disease, pulmonary hypertension, or sleep-disordered breathing. For patients requiring long-term oxygen therapy, a practice that provides structured home oxygen review and offers pulmonary rehabilitation improves outcomes.
preventionStrategies: Stop smoking; it is the single most effective step to prevent COPD and smoking-related hypoxemia.
Treat underlying chronic diseases — asthma, COPD, heart failure — to prevent exacerbations that drive acute hypoxemia.
Receive influenza and pneumococcal vaccination to reduce pneumonia risk, the most common acute cause of hospital hypoxemia.
Lose weight if obese; a 10% body weight reduction measurably improves nocturnal oxygen saturation in obesity hypoventilation syndrome.
Ascend to altitude gradually, allowing 24-48 hours at intermediate altitudes before climbing higher, to permit acclimatisation.
Avoid sedating medications when there is a recognised risk of respiratory depression, or use them under medical supervision with oximetry monitoring.
lifestyleModifications: Use a home pulse oximeter if you have chronic lung disease; knowing your usual baseline allows early recognition of deterioration.
Complete long-term oxygen therapy sessions fully — at least 15 hours per day — if prescribed; adherence determines survival benefit.
Pace activities and use energy-conservation techniques to reduce breathlessness during daily tasks.
Sleep in a semi-recumbent position if lying flat worsens breathlessness from heart failure or obesity.
Attend pulmonary rehabilitation if referred; the improvements in exercise tolerance and quality of life are substantial.
Keep rescue medication (reliever inhaler, diuretics) accessible and know the plan for worsening symptoms.

recommended: A balanced, high-protein diet to maintain respiratory muscle mass in chronic lung disease
Small, frequent meals to reduce diaphragm compression and breathlessness after eating in advanced COPD
Adequate hydration to keep secretions loose and easier to clear
Reduced sodium intake in patients with coexisting heart failure to limit fluid retention and pulmonary congestion
avoid: Large meals that push the diaphragm up and worsen breathlessness in severe COPD
Alcohol, which depresses the respiratory drive and can worsen nocturnal desaturation
Excessive simple carbohydrates, which produce more CO2 per calorie metabolised than fat and can worsen hypercapnia in ventilatory-limited patients

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise is encouraged for people with chronic hypoxemia, ideally under the supervision of a pulmonary rehabilitation programme. Exercise capacity is limited by hypoxemia but the physiological adaptations from training improve peripheral oxygen extraction, reducing the SpO2 drop for a given workload. Patients using supplemental oxygen during exercise should continue it as prescribed. Activities that cause SpO2 to fall below 88% should be reduced in intensity until lung function is better controlled.
dailyManagement: Check SpO2 with a home pulse oximeter at the same time each day and record it in a diary.
Take all prescribed inhalers, diuretics, and other medications consistently — missing doses leads to rapid deterioration.
Know the action plan agreed with your specialist: which SpO2 threshold triggers calling for help and which warrants going directly to an emergency department.
Avoid smoke, chemical fumes, and extremes of cold air, which can all precipitate bronchoconstriction and acute hypoxemia.
Request an annual influenza vaccine and, if not yet had it, a one-time pneumococcal vaccine.
prognosis: Prognosis depends entirely on the cause and severity of hypoxemia. Acute hypoxemia from a reversible cause such as pneumonia, pulmonary oedema, or a COPD exacerbation that is treated promptly resolves without lasting deficit in the majority of patients. Acute respiratory distress syndrome carries a hospital mortality of approximately 30-40%, though survivors typically recover lung function substantially within 6-12 months. Chronic hypoxemia from COPD or pulmonary fibrosis follows the trajectory of the underlying disease: for COPD with resting hypoxemia, long-term oxygen therapy reduces 5-year mortality by approximately 30-50%. Severe, uncorrected chronic hypoxemia leads to secondary pulmonary hypertension, right heart failure (cor pulmonale), and polycythaemia over years, each of which independently worsens prognosis. Nocturnal hypoxemia from sleep apnea treated with CPAP carries an excellent prognosis with near-normalisation of cardiovascular risk.
recoveryTimeline: Recovery from an acute hypoxemia episode typically follows the course of the underlying illness. Pneumonia-related hypoxemia usually resolves within 1-2 weeks of effective antibiotic treatment, with SpO2 normalising before radiographic clearing. Pulmonary embolism-related hypoxemia often improves within 24-48 hours of anticoagulation as perfusion to lung segments is partially restored. Cardiogenic pulmonary oedema responds within hours of diuresis and oxygen therapy. Post-ARDS recovery of full lung function takes 3-6 months and sometimes longer, though most survivors achieve normal spirometry at 12 months. Chronic hypoxemia from COPD does not reverse, but exacerbation recovery to the individual's baseline typically takes 2-6 weeks.
complications: Secondary pulmonary hypertension — chronic hypoxic vasoconstriction raises pulmonary artery pressure, progressively straining the right ventricle.
Cor pulmonale and right heart failure — the right ventricle hypertrophies and ultimately fails from sustained elevated pulmonary pressures in uncorrected chronic hypoxemia.
Polycythaemia — elevated erythropoietin from renal oxygen sensing drives excess red blood cell production, raising blood viscosity and stroke risk.
Cognitive impairment and mood disorders — chronic cerebral hypoxia causes measurable deficits in attention, memory, and executive function, along with increased rates of depression and anxiety.
Cardiac arrhythmias — hypoxemia lowers the threshold for ventricular and atrial arrhythmias, particularly in the presence of underlying structural heart disease.
End-organ damage — prolonged severe hypoxemia, if uncorrected, can cause myocardial ischaemia, acute kidney injury, and liver hypoxia.

supportResources.name: NHS — Low blood oxygen levels (hypoxia)
supportResources.description: Patient-facing NHS guidance on the signs, causes, and management of low blood oxygen.

supportResources.name: NHLBI — COPD and Oxygen Therapy
supportResources.description: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute patient guidance on long-term oxygen therapy in COPD and related hypoxemia.

supportResources.name: British Lung Foundation — Using oxygen at home
supportResources.description: Practical guidance on home oxygen equipment, routines, and safety for people with chronic hypoxemia.

sources.title: NICE NG191 — COVID-19 rapid guideline: managing COVID-19 (oxygen targets for hypoxaemia)
Guérin C et al. Prone Positioning in Severe Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (PROSEVA). NEJM, 2013
Frat J-P et al. High-Flow Oxygen through Nasal Cannula in Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure (FLORALI). NEJM, 2015

confusedWithConditions.name: Anaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anaemia reduces blood oxygen-carrying capacity and causes tissue hypoxia without necessarily lowering arterial PaO2 or SpO2, whereas hypoxemia is defined by low arterial oxygen tension; the two can coexist but require different treatments.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma is one cause of hypoxemia during exacerbations, but a patient with asthma is not the same as a patient with hypoxemia; asthma must be treated at its source, and SpO2 may be entirely normal between attacks.

confusedWithConditions.name: Heart failure
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Heart failure causes hypoxemia through pulmonary oedema, but breathlessness in heart failure can occur with a normal SpO2 from reduced cardiac output alone; diuretics address the cardiac cause rather than oxygen alone.

confusedWithConditions.name: COPD
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: COPD is a common driver of hypoxemia but many COPD patients maintain normal SpO2 until late disease; hypoxemia is a complication of COPD, not synonymous with it.

relatedConditions.name: COPD
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common cause of chronic hypoxemia in adults; progressive airflow obstruction drives V/Q mismatch and eventual resting hypoxemia.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: The leading acute infectious cause of hypoxemia through alveolar consolidation and intrapulmonary shunt.

relatedConditions.name: Heart failure
relatedConditions.relevance: Elevated pulmonary venous pressure causes pulmonary oedema and hypoxemia; correction of hypoxemia requires treating the cardiac cause.

relatedConditions.name: Sleep apnea
relatedConditions.relevance: Repeated upper airway obstruction during sleep produces episodic nocturnal hypoxemia, driving cardiovascular complications over time.

relatedConditions.name: Anaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Anaemia and hypoxemia both reduce tissue oxygen delivery and frequently coexist in critically ill patients, compounding hypoxia.

faqs.question: What is hypoxemia and how does it differ from hypoxia?
faqs.answer: Hypoxemia means low oxygen in arterial blood, measured as PaO2 below 80 mmHg or SpO2 below 95%. Hypoxia means the tissues are not getting enough oxygen. Hypoxemia is the most common cause of hypoxia, but you can have hypoxia with normal SpO2 — for example in severe anaemia, where blood oxygen is adequate but red cell count is too low to deliver enough.

faqs.question: What is a normal oxygen saturation level?
faqs.answer: A normal SpO2 reading on a pulse oximeter is 95-100% for a healthy adult at sea level. Readings of 94% or below at rest are considered low and warrant assessment. In people with known COPD, a baseline of 88-92% may be acceptable and is intentionally maintained to avoid suppressing the respiratory drive.

faqs.question: What does it feel like to have low oxygen levels?
faqs.answer: Mild hypoxemia often causes no symptoms at rest and may first be noticed as unusual breathlessness on exertion, faster-than-normal heart rate, or fatigue. As saturation falls further, headache, difficulty concentrating, restlessness, and shortness of breath at rest develop. Severe hypoxemia causes confusion, cyanosis of the lips, and can rapidly progress to loss of consciousness.

faqs.question: What SpO2 level requires emergency treatment?
faqs.answer: An SpO2 below 90% at rest is generally a medical emergency. In people with COPD who normally run low, a reading below 88% or a drop of more than 4% from their usual baseline warrants urgent assessment. Any SpO2 below 85% is a serious emergency regardless of the person's history.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of hypoxemia?
faqs.answer: The most common cause in hospital patients is ventilation-perfusion mismatch from conditions such as pneumonia, COPD, pulmonary embolism, and asthma. Intrapulmonary shunt from fluid-filled alveoli in pulmonary oedema or ARDS is the next most common. Outside hospital, COPD exacerbations, pneumonia, and heart failure account for most acute hypoxemia. Nocturnal desaturation from sleep apnea is the most common cause of intermittent hypoxemia in the community.

faqs.question: Can you have low oxygen and not know it?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mild hypoxemia, particularly when it develops gradually as in chronic COPD, may cause no obvious symptoms until SpO2 falls below about 88-90%. Nocturnal hypoxemia from sleep apnea occurs during sleep and is rarely felt as breathlessness. This is why pulse oximetry screening is routine in people with known lung or heart disease.

faqs.question: Does supplemental oxygen always fix hypoxemia?
faqs.answer: Supplemental oxygen corrects most hypoxemia caused by V/Q mismatch, where inspired oxygen reaches poorly ventilated areas and raises PaO2. It is less effective for dominant shunt, where blood bypasses ventilated alveoli entirely — as in ARDS or severe pneumonia. These cases require positive-pressure ventilation to recruit collapsed alveoli.

faqs.question: How is hypoxemia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A pulse oximeter provides a quick SpO2 screen, but definitive diagnosis requires an arterial blood gas that measures PaO2 directly. ABG also identifies whether hypoxemia is caused by hypoventilation (raised CO2) or V/Q mismatch and shunt (normal or low CO2). Chest X-ray and, if needed, CT scan identify the structural cause.

faqs.question: What is the target oxygen level for someone with COPD?
faqs.answer: For people with COPD who retain CO2, British and international guidelines target SpO2 88-92% rather than the usual 94-98%. Giving too much oxygen suppresses the hypoxic drive to breathe in these patients, causing CO2 to accumulate further. This makes oxygen therapy in COPD a balance rather than a case of more being better.

faqs.question: What is long-term oxygen therapy and who needs it?
faqs.answer: Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) means using supplemental oxygen at home for at least 15 hours per day. It is indicated when resting PaO2 is persistently at or below 55 mmHg, or below 60 mmHg if there is right heart failure or elevated red cell count. Used consistently, LTOT extends survival in COPD with chronic hypoxemia by approximately 30-50%.

faqs.question: Can exercise cause dangerous oxygen desaturation?
faqs.answer: Exercise-induced desaturation — SpO2 falling to 88% or below on exertion — is common in interstitial lung disease and moderate-to-severe COPD, and is associated with faster disease progression. Pulmonary rehabilitation programmes monitor SpO2 during exercise and titrate supplemental oxygen to maintain acceptable saturations. Exercising with supplemental oxygen is safe and improves exercise tolerance.

faqs.question: What happens to the heart with long-term low oxygen?
faqs.answer: Chronic low oxygen causes sustained constriction of the pulmonary arteries, progressively raising pulmonary blood pressure (pulmonary hypertension). Over time the right ventricle hypertrophies under this increased workload and eventually fails — a condition called cor pulmonale. Long-term oxygen therapy slows this progression when used for at least 15 hours a day.

faqs.question: Can hypoxemia cause brain damage?
faqs.answer: Prolonged severe hypoxemia can cause permanent brain injury. The brain is the most oxygen-sensitive organ; SpO2 below 80-85% sustained for more than a few minutes begins to injure neurons, with hippocampal cells particularly vulnerable. Milder chronic hypoxemia (SpO2 88-92%) causes measurable but often subtle deficits in memory, attention, and executive function that may partially reverse with oxygen therapy.

faqs.question: Is a pulse oximeter accurate for everyone?
faqs.answer: Pulse oximeters are less accurate in people with dark skin pigmentation, where studies show SpO2 may be overestimated by 2-4 percentage points on average. They also lose accuracy with nail polish, cold peripheries, a fast heart rate, and carbon monoxide poisoning (where they read falsely normal). When accuracy matters, an arterial blood gas provides a definitive measurement.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hypoxemia and respiratory failure?
faqs.answer: Hypoxemia refers to low arterial oxygen; respiratory failure means the lungs cannot maintain adequate gas exchange for the body's needs. Type 1 respiratory failure is hypoxemia without elevated CO2, typical of pneumonia or pulmonary embolism. Type 2 respiratory failure combines hypoxemia with elevated CO2 (hypercapnia), indicating the respiratory muscles or drive cannot sustain ventilation, as in severe COPD or neuromuscular disease.

faqs.question: Can low oxygen levels affect sleep?
faqs.answer: Yes. Nocturnal hypoxemia occurs in obstructive sleep apnea, obesity hypoventilation syndrome, and advanced COPD — often without the person being aware. Each desaturation episode triggers a micro-arousal that fragments sleep, causing daytime sleepiness. Repeated nocturnal desaturation drives cardiovascular strain over time. Overnight oximetry or a sleep study confirms the diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is high-altitude hypoxemia dangerous?
faqs.answer: Mild altitude hypoxemia during gradual ascent is generally well tolerated and corrects within days as the body acclimatises. Rapid ascent above 3,000 metres risks acute mountain sickness and, in severe cases, high-altitude pulmonary oedema (HAPE), where fluid floods the lungs and SpO2 can fall rapidly. HAPE is life-threatening and treated with immediate descent, supplemental oxygen, and sometimes nifedipine.

faqs.question: How quickly can hypoxemia become life-threatening?
faqs.answer: The speed depends on its cause and the starting SpO2. Complete airway obstruction causes unconsciousness in 3-5 minutes. Sudden massive pulmonary embolism or tension pneumothorax can produce critical hypoxemia within minutes. In contrast, a slowly worsening pneumonia may take hours to reach dangerous levels. An SpO2 below 80% at rest is life-threatening within minutes to a few hours without intervention.

faqs.question: What is prone positioning for hypoxemia?
faqs.answer: Prone positioning turns a mechanically ventilated patient face-down for 16 hours at a time. Gravity shifts blood flow toward the front of the lungs, where ventilation is better, dramatically improving oxygenation in severe ARDS. The PROSEVA trial showed it reduced 28-day mortality from 32.8% to 16% in patients with PaO2/FiO2 below 150. It requires a specialised team to perform safely.

faqs.question: Can anxiety cause low oxygen levels?
faqs.answer: Anxiety and panic attacks cause rapid, shallow breathing (hyperventilation), which usually raises blood oxygen slightly rather than lowering it. The SpO2 does not fall during a panic attack in a person with healthy lungs. However, anxiety can feel like breathlessness and mimics hypoxemia closely; a pulse oximeter showing a normal SpO2 during a symptom episode provides useful reassurance.

faqs.question: What foods or supplements help with oxygen levels?
faqs.answer: No food or supplement directly raises arterial oxygen saturation in a person with lung disease. Addressing anaemia with iron or B12 if deficient improves oxygen-carrying capacity and tissue delivery. A high-protein diet preserves respiratory muscle mass. Antioxidants such as vitamin C and E may modestly slow progression of some interstitial lung diseases. No supplement replaces medical oxygen therapy when SpO2 is genuinely low.

metaDescription: Hypoxemia means low oxygen in arterial blood, causing breathlessness, confusion and cyanosis. Learn causes, SpO2 targets and oxygen treatment. Doctor-reviewed.
